sentences.text: Good morning.
Good morning.
How are we doing?
Good.
How was your holiday?
It was great.
How was yours?
Very relaxing, thank you for asking.
Good, good.
Kelly, where are you based out of?
I don't think we've had the pleasure of meeting.
Um, I'm in Palm Beach.
OK.
Yeah.
Wonderful,
wonderful.
I work remotely and then I travel usually about once a week to do in-person trainings.
OK, great.
That's awesome.
Well, I'm based out of Atlanta.
It's been pretty cold here for Atlanta.
Has it, how's it been in Palm Beach lately?
Um, it's been in the 70s, which is great.
It's just been really gray.
We have some sun today actually, which is nice, but my husband saw something that in a couple weeks we're about to get weather in like the 20s or 30s.
I don't know if I believe that, so we'll see.
He thinks it's fake, but it's a real sight, so we'll see.
Yeah, my husband saw that there was, there's calling for like a foot of snow in Atlanta in like a week or two, which is crazy.
I don't believe it.
If it happens, my kids will love it, but let's see if it actually happens.
Yeah, same.
Yeah.
I'm not getting my hopes up.
How are you doing, Liz, this morning?
Um, well,
I'm waiting for triage to call me from the nurses so I can go in because I have been super sick.
So I'm going to more than likely just um be hidden for this call.
I'm not quite camera ready this morning, um, so, but I, you know, I'll, I'll add my two cents when needed.
I'm so sorry.
Well, I hope you feel better.
Feel free to hop off at any time, like, we'll fill you in.
Thanks, I appreciate it.
Absolutely.
And hi, Amanda, how are you?
You hear me?
Yeah.
No I can't now.
I still don't know why my ears are not working.
Mhm
All right, so everyone had a fabulous holiday, relaxing.
Yeah, awesome.
All right.
Well, I'm really excited to have you all here today.
Uh, I met two weeks ago, actually.
It's crazy how fast time flies, uh, with Kirsten and Liz to talk about, uh, just the rollout for training for the Rainwater team.
Um, I'm the customer trainer here at Apartment IQ, and so I'll be working really closely with you all so that you feel comfortable, confident on leveraging our tool.
Talk about the resources that we have, and then any kind of deliverables or resources that you'll need to feel successful.
Um, just out of curiosity, Kirsten, I know you took a peek at the help center.
Um, I think we sent over a pre-recorded training for that as well.
Any feedback, comments, or things that you're on top of mind that you would need, uh, going forward.
So, um, I think this is kind of why I wanted to have this session, I guess, just to like mainly Amanda and Kelly ask any questions and get some preliminary information to you.
And I did have a question, um, and maybe Liz, this is more for you.
Do we have a, a start date or an implementation date established now?
Um, the Property management really wants to push for a January 15th go live.
However, we need to provide apartment IQ with individual users, their first name, last name, emails, and what roles they're assigned to.

And, um, we're trying to get that information, but Kirsten, you and I are meeting with, um, Amanda on Friday now because she's still on PTO on Thursday to to discuss what we think the rules would be and to just to discuss that.
So I don't think we're gonna be able to get that, that to them in time, but the property management is flexible to maybe a February 1st start date.
So it looks like it'll probably be February 1st, just based on what information we can get to apartment IQ in order for them to get everything up and running.
Perfect.

Yeah, I know that our team is starting to work on building out uh some of your uh market surveys there, and just for uh Kelly and Amanda, these are the user roles that they were speaking to, so 100% correct.
What we'll do is we'll add an individualized users, so that way you can customize what level of visibility they will have.
Typically for property management, it kind of depends on your team's preference.
They'll either be an editor or a read-only user depending on whether or not you want them to have access to change competitive properties or not.
Uh read-only just essentially gives them visibility.
They can filter, sort, extract, uh, automated reports and pull reports instantly.
Uh, they just can't change anything without somebody who is an editor, manager, or admin making those adjustments for them.
So what my understanding is, Jamie, correct me if I'm wrong, they have all the properties right now, the property list so they're building that out.
It's just on us and we're trying to decipher because we have to give each user at the property that.

Access to that property and um so that's kind of more the tricky part we're trying to reconcile the radixs list list with um like with a sales force or something to get their properties and their um positions so that's kind of where we're at now.
Yeah, and typically I I'm sure uh Larissa has shared this with you, but there's usually like a user template that you can complete, and we'll get those uploaded for you.

Uh, the great news is if you ever need to make adjustments to user permissions or want to change somebody's level of um visibility that you're seeing here, you all will have access to do that as admins or managers, so you can make adjustments for them.
OK, so for today's session, let's just first and foremost talk about what apartment IQ is, so that way we can start there.
Let me just go back in my little uh presentation here real quick.
So to kick us off first and foremost, let me show you who your team's customer success manager is.
So Larissa is your team's customer success manager.
She's here.
For really the roll out of your, uh, overall account.
She'll be here for any setup questions and things like that and just really the overall success of your team over time.
And then we also have a support team and I'll show you how to reach out to them as well.
Everybody on your team who has access to apartment IQ will also have access to our support team.
All right, so first and foremost, when I typically lead these trainings, I'll talk through what the objectives of the call will be.
We'll first and foremost define apartment IQ.
We're gonna walk through all the different reports.

We'll spend the majority of our time on the market survey report because we that property managers really spend about 80 to 90% of their time there, uh, and then we'll do a quick little review of all the other reports, also show them how to access the help center with all those great resources, and how to reach out to our support team.
So apartment IQ, essentially what we are is we are an automated market survey tool.

And so instead of you all having to manually go to people's websites, call around for pricing, availability, our tool searches your websites on a nightly basis, as well as your competitors' websites, and pulls all that information in for you so that you can sort it, filter it and extract automated reports.
Without having any kind of human error associated with those reports.
Now the great thing about our tool is we are pulling from 100% publicly available data.

So rather than having to worry about the private data sharing laws, we're updating this information every 24 hours from those public data sources that you're really able to get the most up to-date information that's out there.

We're currently tracking upwards of 3 million properties, and we don't require your competitors to be a part of our ecosystem, kind of like radix does, simply because we're not doing any kind of data swapping.
We're pulling it right from their websites, uh, and so a lot of people have really enjoyed the time savings that come with leveraging apartment IQ.
Any questions here from you all?
OK.
And we talked about the user roles, so we're gonna go ahead and jump in so I can talk to you about how to navigate apartment IQ.
I'm gonna leverage this demo account for today since our team is still actively building out your team's instance.
So this is my demo.
When we actually do the trainings, I will leverage your account cause it should be built out by then.
So first and foremost, when your users log in, they're going to land on this My Properties page.
Now, if I'm a property manager and I only oversee 1200 Broadway, you may only provision access to that property.
So instead of them seeing all 150 of your properties, they would just see that one.
OK, so whatever visibility you give them is what they'll see when they log in.
And when they actually enter into a market survey, there's several different reports that they'll have access to.
As I mentioned, we'll spend like probably 15 to 20 minutes right here on the market survey really digging into the filters, customizations, automations that the users can leverage here.
Then we'll kind of walk through at a high level some of the other reports.
So the floor plan report is going to be more of like a, let's take a look at how your bed types are performing or your individualized floor plans.
So it's gonna get more and more granular as we're moving to the right hand side.
The unit's report is going to showcase all of the individualized units.
So that's something that's really um unique about apartment IQ is we inspect websites down to the unit level.

So we're able to see the units that are going to be available across your compet into the next 30 days, providing you with a ton of information about those units from when it's available, the square footage, price per square foot, all those great details.
And then this last one that I'll go into with your teams is the historical section.

Typically for a property manager, I stay really high level here just kind of talking about how they can access information regarding leasing activity and availability, uh, historically and how prices have changed over time.
Um, we find that a lot of folks that are, um, regionals and above love this report explicitly for the level of history that we can go into here.
Uh, we can access up to 3 years of history for your concept.
OK, any questions about navigating before we hop into kind of uh digging into the data we're seeing in the market survey?
Jamie, are you gonna go over um the reports at some point?
Absolutely, absolutely.
OK, so within the market survey itself, we will provide you with some standardized metrics along the left, but every single one of your users has the opportunity to customize this.
Now, we really leave this open for your teams to define what metrics.
are mandatory and standard.

A lot of times teams will choose the metrics and send out like a a message internally to be like, hey, these are the expected metrics to pull in weekly reports, and I'll talk to you about how you can kind of um Set that up and then also establish the norms for pulling reports, but a lot of times for folks in property management, they want to see things like, obviously these top three distance you built total units.
Um, we encourage you to turn on reviews.
This is gonna pull in Google reviews from all of your sites.
Then your teams have the opportunity to customize this.
You can pull in things like rent, net effective rent, rent per square foot if you'd like.
This is gonna give you the average rent across your market for all bed types.
Now if we want to dig into this a little bit more, we'll go down a little further to see it broken down by your ones, your 2s, your 3s.
But there's a lot of options here, advertise occupancy, exposure, availability, vacancy, all the good stuff, and then definitely concessions.
Your teams are gonna love that they don't have to call around for concessions anymore.
Now once all of the metrics are set for them, we can then sort and filter by any metric that you see.
Every single one of these metrics has a definition associated with them, so they can hover over them to get a little bit more information.
If at any time they need more information.
We strongly urge them to check the help center.
We actually, I partnered very closely with our product team to create a glossary.

Uh, in that glossary, we have definitions, examples, uh, we break it down a little bit further so that way if they have, um, they need a calculation or they want to provide that information to somebody externally, we'll provide all that to you right here in the help center.
So definitely encourage you to check this out, and we've got all sorts of getting started guides, our product updates in there, and then our frequently asked questions.
All right, how are we feeling so far?
Jamie, I have a question on the other page where you have the customized metrics available.
Um, well, not really the customized, this page is perfect.
So where it says your leases in the last 7 days, how are you determining that with public information?
Yeah,
absolutely.
So we commonly get questions about these last 3, so I usually dive into these really explicitly.
So applications is going to refer to the units that have been removed from your website in the last 7 days.
Now apartment IQ is 1 day behind, so you'll see the date in the top left hand corner.
We inspect your website overnight for any changes that have occurred.
So we look to your website and we say, oh, there's actually been one unit that's been removed from the website in the last 7 days.
We can actually see which unit that is, the price that it's going for, and even the history of that unit.
So we can actually click on the number here to see what was the price when it was officially listed on your website.
And then what was the price when it was removed from your website, um, and we define it as an application simply because we know that some of those renters won't be ultimately approved.
They might be declined and that unit might go back online.
So we're going to give it a 7 day window here from when it's removed, and then if it goes back online, we will post it as a relised unit for you.
If it stays offline for 7 days, we're gonna count it as an official lease.
We assume that if that unit stayed offline for more than 7 days, that renter was approved.
OK, so in the case where an apartment is taken offline because of damage, it's gonna be considered to be an application, and if it stays off for 7 days.
OK, how do you account for things like you're on a lease of property and they don't put all of their inventory online at the same time?
That's a great question.
So we typically get questions about that regarding our advertised occupancy metrics.
So to kind of explain how apartment IQ is projecting your advertised occupancy, it's all based off of what is being provided on your website.
So for a stabilized property, let's say that I currently have 21 beds that are available.
I might not want to display all 20 of those simply because I want to move the units that are currently vacant or that are coming up for renewal soon.
I can hide those units with filters.
Apartment IQ will see those units regardless of the filtering, as long as they're published to your website.
Now, if they're withheld from your website for any reason, like they're a down unit, they're a unit that's under construction, it's a unit that's being renovated, removed for damage.
We don't know that they exist simply because they're not published anywhere, right?
So we're pulling that information right from that public data source.
And so in that case, we wouldn't be able to apply that to the occupancy metrics.

Now, with lease up properties specifically, we are currently working on a new calculation because we know that a lot of times with brand new lease ups, you may not publish every single one of your units right away.

Sometimes you'll publish half of your stock right away or just publish 25% of that property, and so we're working on a new uh calculation explicitly for lease up properties, and I can definitely have Larissa pass information along once that's officially released.
I actually have a meeting with our um product manager this afternoon, and I can ask him about it too, to see like where they're at with that.
Perfect, thank you.
Yeah.

I have a really silly because I'm still naive to this industry question when you say um is showing the Google reviews, is that just Google or does that pull from like Zillow and apartments.com and all that like there are the reviews all encompassing or are they just Specific.
That's a really good question.
So it's all coming right from Google.
You can actually click on here and it'll pull in this Google review.
So currently we're not pulling it from uh Zillow or apartments.com for for those reviews.
All right, thank you.
Yeah,
absolutely.
Um, any questions regarding any of the other metrics?

I definitely during the trainings cover those three explicitly, and we talk about advertised occupancy explicitly, what they can expect it to include, exclude, and if they have questions regarding their occupancy metrics, we always talk about how we can reach out to the support team.
Any of these metrics here can be contacted via the suggested update.
So this button will allow our team to know what metrics you have questions about.
So let's say for example, my advertised occupancy, I know it to be actually at 87%, and I'm like, how are you guys getting 92.3%?
What we can do is send in a message to our product team.
I would choose the property, so my property is 1200 Broadway.

The metric that is in question would be the advertised occupancy metric, and then I can provide my suggested percentage and any kind of comments that would help our team to diagnose it a little bit more, they'll respond back via email with any kind of information that they can provide to you on that.
OK, how are we feeling about the metrics at the top?
I would say this is probably the section that most people have the majority of questions on is how we're pulling this data, we're getting it calculating it.
OK, one last thing that I will mention is any of these properties we can actually click on them, and it will provide us with a ton of information regarding the performance of that property.
So we can see the property's website, we can also see any units that are available that have leased, and actually a unit mix of all of them.
You'll see all of the projected unit mix for that property and all of the most recent leased units for that property and availability, so it's kind of giving you like a a rent roll of sorts here.
Any of this is filterable.

So you can filter any uh section here to see those units in order, makes it really easy to identify what units they don't have published to, because if there's a unit that's missing in here, like I don't see 04 and 05, you can probably denote that that's perhaps employee housing or a unit that's um being renovated or something like that.
This is so cool.
Is there a way to see if another one of our competitors is also in this ecosystem as well?
That's a great question.

We currently don't share what um Which of our customers are within apartment IQ just simply because it comes from public data, but that has been asked several times like to be able to see which ownership group belongs to and things like that.
I'll definitely pass it along to our product team for feedback.

Well, I'm just realizing how incredible this is compared to some of the things that we've been using, so it definitely is going to give a competitive advantage and I would be curious to know which of our comps also have that advantage.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Definitely, let me, uh, I'll definitely pass that along to our our team.
Awesome.
So just one final thing here, all of these are sortable.
So one common thing that a lot of people like to sort by is applications, just kind of see their positioning in the market that week.
So all of these you can sort by to see where you're currently sitting.
So right now my property is 7th out of 10th in the market for the total number of applications.
I can sort by literally anything here that I'd like, um, just to see where I'm currently positioned in the market.
All right.
There's 4 sections on this market survey.
The second one here is pricing and availability.
Now we're getting a little bit more detailed here.
It's broken down by bed type.
Now, the biggest thing that I like to impress upon for your teams is the toggle here for filters.
It's really crucial that they set the filter to the right toggle depending on their needs for the day.

Now, if you're looking just to get an average of your bed type, so if I wanted to see what is the average rent for my studios across the market, I can set the toggle the average and see all of the 40 units that I currently have available.
We're going to average all of those metrics for you and tell you what that average price, average square footage is, all the good stuff.
We then have two other options.
Lowest is going to look at the values for a singular unit.
So out of my 40 units, we're going to find and identify the cheapest one and tell you every metric associated with that one unit.
Whereas absolute minimum is going to look at the cheapest unit, the smallest unit, uh, the unit with the lowest concession value, and pull those in for you.
Does that make sense?

So averaging absolute minimum essentially is gonna look at those all 40 units and average them, or take the the lowest value, whereas that um lowest rent is gonna look at that cheapest 1 unit and pull all the metrics in for that one.
So it just kind of depends on what you're looking at.
We've had people request both, so we've just decided to provide that to everybody as an option.
And then from here you literally can sort this again if you'd like.
So if you're like, OK, where am I positioned currently for rent pricing in my market for studios, I can sort that to see I'm currently in 10th.

It's going off of the screen, so I at any time have the opportunity to sort it the other way, high to low, low to high, or I can even pin my property, so that way I never lose a visibility into it as well.
And then uh down here will be all the fees, deposits and amenities.
Now, one thing that I do want to impress upon your team is that fees, deposits, and amenities, they're not on your website, will actually look to ILS sources for them.
A lot of our uh folks that we work with like to kind of use this as an audit.

I worked with another large enterprise client and they were like, oh man, like we have some work to do with our ILS data, uh, because, for example, they didn't have like their parking fees displayed or like their utility fees.
And so if it's not displayed, we can't pull it in, OK?
Now, you can request our team to do an override, but it will not actually change anything on your ILS sources.
So we typically recommend that folks do more of like an audit of their ILS data, just to make sure that it's updated, uh, that it's accurate, and then it's then pulling in to apartment IQ accurately.
And if there's anything that we need to make adjustments to, we're happy to review that.
Um, which ILS, um, sources are you using?
that ones or they're provided by the organization?
That's a really good question.
So we actually look up to like 15 different ILS sources, but we always look to the most common ones first, right?
So we're looking at the common denominator there, and I'm sure you know like the most common ILS source.
Uh, like apartments.com, things like that, we're gonna look to, um, and we're always gonna look for, as I mentioned, the common denominator.
If there's something, uh, that looks off or strange or is only presented in one location, we're not gonna pull it in.
OK, and if it's all over the board, are you averaging or you're just gonna stick with the apartments.com one?
I believe they look to the one that again is most common, but again, if it's, if it's not correct, we can definitely make adjustments for you.
Any questions on that?
OK, and then down here is those uh amenities as well as um that are in unit as well as community wide.
My favorite thing about this section is it's actually gonna show you the total number of properties in this comp average section that have that amenity.
So if it's something that's really unique.

To your property, it'll give your leasing specialists an advantage to be able to speak to that, like, hey, we're the only property in this area that has air conditioning, which I hope that's not the case, uh, or we're the only, uh, property in the area that has a pool.
Uh, there's only a couple here that are kind of fitting the bill for you.
Uh, so definitely leverage this, uh, to help your teams just kind of find those little value adds, uh, during the sales process there.
OK.
Now before I talk about any automations, what questions do we have or any resources that are coming to mind that you think would be essential here?
I'm looking at you, Kelly.
I mean this is great so far.
I haven't reviewed the video.
Um, I don't know if there's a link to that that I can review the video that you referenced earlier.

I'd love to see that, but I mean so far a lot of this is self-explanatory except for the questions of how you're pulling the data, and it sounds like you're gonna go over that with people, so I think it is fantastic so far.
Great.
Awesome.
OK, so we do have different reports that your team will have the opportunity to schedule.
Now, going back to that initial comment that I made regarding the metrics, there are two different reports that we offer.
The first is this PDF version.

Currently, this is a static report, but very soon it's all going to be based off of these customizations, hence why I'm encouraging your team to kind of set some standards there around which metrics should and should not be included in reports that are pulled for any kind of key stakeholder.
What we can actually do is within a PDF report, your team has the opportunity to set this and forget it.

They plug in their email, they choose a frequency, weekly, monthly, quarterly for property management, the typical standard is weekly, and then you can choose whatever day you'd like it to schedule on.

They can also come in here and pull one right away, so that way if they're in the middle of a conversation about pricing, and they want the most up to-date information possible, you'd be able to pull this during that meeting and be able to see that data quickly.
So it's going to show that 7 day trend will provide the uh time stamp up here in the top left.
It's going to show you all key things like the trends in rent, the trends in NER, trends in net effective per square foot.
We'll then dig into how your bed types are performing.
How are the studios, ones, twos, how have they adjusted in the last week?

And then typically the teams love this last page because it's gonna show them any kind of new concession, concessions that have ended, and then any kind of restrictions associated with those concessions, so they can see, oh, OK, so 805 LEA has a uh concession running right now, but they only have very select units that it's applicable to, uh, and then you can be able to speak to that and leverage that in sales conversations.
Now we do offer a second type of reports.
This one is an Excel version.
This can be scheduled as well.
This is actually going to provide your teams with an exact replica of this market survey that we're looking at right now, and it's all based off of customizations.
So any kind of filters, metrics, sorting options that they include will be pulled into this report, and it's also actually going to provide us with 4 other reports that folks can leverage.
I would say that the majority of folks in property management really just care about that market survey.
They may want to schedule this so that way they can see uh that market survey pulled into a report for them so they don't have to manually create this anymore.
Uh, it's got all of those key metrics, pricing for them, uh, pulled right into a nice tidy report.
Now, so for some of your folks that are regionals above, they might want things that are a little bit more complex and intricate in terms of the data.
We're going to be able to provide you with a 30, 60, and 90 day summary report, showcasing how pricing has changed, availability has changed.
We'll also show you key metrics over the last 7 days for bed data.
Floor plan data and provide you with a unit level report dating back to October of 2020.
So I'll show you all of the leasing statuses for your compet rolled up into a nice little report here.
This really helps with like asset teams uh and just pricing teams when they're working on uh setting uh rents for uh units as well.
Jamie, would you be able to share like sample copies like these so that we can dig through at our leisure?
Thank you.
Yeah, this is really cool.
Absolutely.
Yeah, and if you have any questions about these, just let me know.
I'm happy to spin up a quick little video walking through it as well.
Cool.
All right.
The last thing, I know this is like kind of information overload today, but the edit comps functionality, this will only be available to folks that you provision it to have availability to, right?
Read only users cannot change concepts.
So if you decide as a team that your property managers should not have access, they won't see this button.
If you decide that you'd like to give your teams access to make adjustments, uh, you can set them as an editor or above.
Now it's very easy to change the concepts.
We're going to provide you with the list of properties that we're currently tracking along the top.
You'll see them on the map view and where your property currently is.
We'll also show you some properties that that your teams may want to consider including, and we'll provide them with what we refer to as a comp score.
It'll tell them how similar those properties in your area are to yours based off of the metrics that you're seeing over here to the right.

So distance, for footage, pricing, all the good stuff, and we're gonna roll that up for you so you can see all of the scores if you will, uh, very easy to remove, press the X, click add, or even search for a property by name.
So I know that you had mentioned before lease ups.
Now, one thing that's really important in regards to how apartment IQ works is we can only track a property if it has live leasing.

So if you have a property that's being built on the street that does not have a website yet, has not started their leasing process, we'll need to wait until that property has officially turned on their online leasing for us to track it simply because that's how we get our information.
So if you go to search for a property, you don't see it, perhaps it's brand new, can always request our team to build it.

This happens very infrequently because we have, as I mentioned, upwards of 3 million properties currently, but for those new lease ups you may need to let us know and we can build them out for your team.
What questions do we have here?
Who's making the decision on access?
Is that um Amanda Hellman's team?
I don't know, Kirsten, if you know that.
Yeah, we're, I'm meeting with Amanda and I put Kirsten on the call as well um on Friday.
Kelly, would you like to be a part of that as well because we're gonna decide which level gets what access.
While I would love that, I feel like that might get a little overstepping.
I just want to put my two.
And that I do feel like it the the access would make sense in my opinion to give to the regional manager for editing comps.
I don't think the um onsite team that's something the on-site team would agree with the regional manager about anyway um that's just my opinion.
I just wanna throw that.
OK, that's great.
I will make that note and bring that up on Friday.
Thank you.
Yeah, and again it's totally up to your teams.
We do find that that's really helpful to kind of mitigate people changing things left and right.

Uh, what I will say is having the regionals have access provides them with an awesome opportunity to have those really great conversations with property managers, so that way they can explore what is currently in their concept that they're tracking perhaps with radix, or What could we expand to or perhaps what might we want to change from what we were previously tracking?
I know that I've worked with teams.
I had somebody tell me, I haven't changed my concept in the last 10 years.
We're like, let's take a look at what we've got in here, see if there's anything new or things that you might want to change.
So just really provide your teams with a ton of resources, data and information to make those adjustments.
Perfect.
OK.
So as I mentioned, we're gonna spend the majority of our time here.
Again, I find that property managers pretty much this is the report that they go to.
Um, I also strongly encourage that folks come in here every day for five-ish minutes a day.
Right?
Unlike radixs, unlike manual processes, there's nothing really for them to do in terms of hunting down information, calling up their competitors.
We strongly encourage them to get in here for five-ish minutes so that they can just get a taste of how the market has adjusted, so they can provide excellent data for those internal communications.
Um, we also find setting up those automated reports is crucial to the success of having that data at their fingertips.
OK, so I'm gonna go at a high level for the rest of the reports just to give you a taste of how these reports work.
Uh, the floor plan report has a toggle.
